section_1_metadata.going_concern_note: Directors explicitly stated NO going concern doubt for the first time in years. Company recorded profit for second consecutive year. However accumulated losses of ₦64.78 billion remain and net current liabilities of ₦122.45 billion remain a structural risk. Prior years had going concern flags. This is a significant inflection point.
section_1_metadata.franchise_area: Federal Capital Territory (FCT) Abuja, Niger, Kogi, Nasarawa States
section_1_metadata.ownership_history_note: KANN Utility acquired 60% at privatisation (Nov 2013). CEC Africa acquired majority of KANN (2020). UCTL became controlling party (Dec 2021). Jeolan International acquired 60% in May 2023 — current parent. Repeated ownership churn signals governance instability and investor flight risk.
section_1_metadata.regulatory_framework: NERC / MYTO / Transitional Electricity Market (TEM) rules effective Feb 2015

revenue_breakdown.note: Electricity sales grew 78% (₦177.8bn → ₦316.9bn). But ₦284.7 billion — 47.3% of total revenue — came from NERC tariff shortfall awards, not from customers actually paying bills. This is a critical Vitics signal: nearly HALF of AEDC's 2024 revenue is a paper accounting entry, not cash collected.

section_2_income_statement.gross_profit_note: Swing from ₦5.7 billion LOSS in 2023 to ₦67.7 billion profit in 2024. Driven entirely by Band A tariff increase (April 2024) and tariff shortfall recognition — not by operational improvement.
section_2_income_statement.impairment_note: New impairment charge of ₦11.7bn in 2024 after a ₦39.5bn reversal in 2023. Signals deteriorating receivables quality — the big 2023 reversal was accounting optimism that is now being unwound.

finance_costs_breakdown.note: Net NBET/ONEM interest of ₦23.8 billion almost wipes out operating profit. The company is essentially paying its power supplier compound interest on unpaid bills — a debt spiral that cannot be solved by tariff increases alone.

section_2_income_statement.profit_note: ₦781 million profit on ₦601 billion revenue = 0.13% net margin. Technically profitable but economically fragile. Any tariff reversal or shortfall non-payment collapses this instantly.
section_2_income_statement.total_comprehensive_income_note: ₦73.7 billion total comprehensive income is almost entirely (99%) a non-cash revaluation of distribution network assets — a balance sheet accounting event, not cash generation.

equity.equity_note: Equity tripled from ₦54.5bn to ₦128.2bn — but 150% of equity (₦192.97bn) is revaluation reserve from asset revaluations. Strip that out and the company has accumulated losses of ₦64.8bn and share capital of ₦15 million. This equity is largely paper wealth, not distributable cash.

section_3_balance_sheet.net_current_liabilities_note: Net current liabilities of ₦122.4 billion (2023: ₦112.7 billion) — WORSENED despite revenue surge. This means AEDC is more technically insolvent in working capital terms than a year ago despite the tariff windfall. The trade payables to NBET/ONEM (₦222bn) are the weight that never lifts.
section_3_balance_sheet.debt_ratio_note: Improvement from 5.78 to 2.99 is real but driven by revaluation, not debt paydown.

section_4_cash_flow.cash_flow_note: Operating cash flow of ₦25.9 billion is genuinely positive and improving (+65% yoy). Cash position more than doubled from ₦11.1bn to ₦24.6bn. This is the most encouraging signal in the entire report — AEDC is actually collecting more cash than before. But ₦12.6bn of the ₦24.6bn cash is ring-fenced (restricted) for CBN VAT remittance and meter asset funding — actual free cash is ~₦12bn.

section_5_revenue_analysis.prepaid_trend_note: Prepaid share of electricity sales FELL from 39.8% to 33.0%. This is counterintuitive — normally prepaid grows as metering improves. Possible explanation: Band A tariff shock pushed metered (postpaid) high-end customers to consume more, growing postpaid faster. Or prepaid customers disconnected/went solar. Warrants Vitics flag.

tariff_shortfall_cumulative_history.note: ₦285 billion tariff shortfall in 2024 alone — more than all prior years combined. This is the government effectively subsidising AEDC's inability to collect cost-reflective tariffs from lower-band customers while Band A customers pay full rates. The shortfall is netted against NBET payables, meaning no cash changes hands.

section_5_revenue_analysis.customer_contributed_assets_note: Dramatic fall from ₦2.2bn to ₦31m. Caused by April 2023 NERC order requiring DISCOs to repay meter costs to customers via energy credits rather than recognising meter funding as revenue. Consumers who pre-funded meters are now being reimbursed via bill credits — policy change benefiting consumers.

section_6_trade_receivables.ecl_trend_note: ECL rate improved from 29% to 24% — AEDC claims better receivables quality. But gross receivables surged ₦82bn (+47%) in one year, driven by Band A tariff hike. Higher bills = higher receivables = higher absolute impairment even if the rate looks better. The absolute charge rose ₦11.3bn.
section_6_trade_receivables.critical_finding: 84% of gross receivables are over 300 days old. ₦137.9 billion is over 361 days — more than a year overdue. AEDC is accumulating a mountain of uncollectable legacy debt that grows faster than it can be written off or collected. This is the defining structural crisis of the Nigerian DISCO model.

receivables_by_customer_type_2024.unmetered_non_md_low_tier_note: ₦157.7 billion — 62% of all gross receivables — owed by unmetered low-tier customers. These are the households that CANNOT pay because they are estimated billed, dispute amounts, or are simply too poor. This is the socioeconomic fault line in AEDC's collections.

cbn_loan_detail.note: Total CBN intervention ₦78 billion drawn. AEDC has been on CBN life support since 2018. The loans carry 14-22% interest — expensive for a company that cannot generate cash. These are not being repaid from operations; they are being refinanced or extended.

section_7_debt_and_borrowings.nbet_payables_note: AEDC owes NBET/ONEM ₦222 billion — up ₦53bn in one year despite ₦284bn tariff shortfall netting. The cost of energy (₦518bn) vastly exceeds what AEDC can collect from customers (₦317bn electricity sales). The gap is filled by shortfall recognition and CBN loans — not by actual collection.
section_7_debt_and_borrowings.map_liability_note: Meter Asset Provider liability tripled from ₦5.5bn to ₦15.6bn. Customers pre-funded 14 MAP entities to install meters — AEDC must repay them via energy credits. This is growing fast as metering scales up, creating a hidden future obligation.
section_7_debt_and_borrowings.related_party_note: Due to Jeolan International (parent) ₦5.8bn — management fees accrued but unpaid. KANN legacy payable ₦7.6bn still sitting on books. Company is paying parent from scarce collections while owing NBET billions.

section_8_metering_and_operations.metering_note: 91,372 customers metered in 2024 — positive momentum. Collections by May 2025 were up 63% yoy (₦153bn vs ₦96bn same period 2024). Smart metering and meter replacement are the company's stated path out of the structural crisis. But the unmetered base remains massive — ₦157.7bn in receivables from unmetered low-tier customers.

collection_efficiency_signals.trend_note: Post-period data (May 2025) shows real improvement. This is the most forward-looking positive signal in the document. If metering continues at pace, 2025 could be a genuine turning point.

section_9_tariff_and_regulatory.tariff_framework: Multi Year Tariff Order (MYTO) under NERC
section_9_tariff_and_regulatory.major_tariff_event_2024: April 2024 — NERC raised Band A tariff to cost-reflective levels
section_9_tariff_and_regulatory.band_a_tariff_note: Band A customers (high-end, typically commercial, government, upper residential with more stable supply) now pay full cost-reflective tariffs. Lower band customers remain subsidised. This bifurcation is the defining regulatory event of 2024 and explains AEDC's revenue surge.
section_9_tariff_and_regulatory.tariff_shortfall_mechanism: NERC awards shortfall orders annually. Shortfalls are netted against NBET payables — no cash changes hands. In 2024 shortfall was ₦285bn, awarded and recognised as revenue.

capmi_scheme.description: Credited Advance Payment for Metering Implementation — customers advance funds for meter procurement, repaid in 36-month instalments plus 12% interest
capmi_scheme.trend: Declining — being replaced by MAP scheme

map_scheme.description: Meter Asset Provider scheme — customers fund meters, DISCOs repay via energy credits. NERC April 2023 order requires energy credit repayment, not cash.

section_9_tariff_and_regulatory.consumer_impact_of_band_a_tariff: Band A customers received massive bill increases in April 2024. Consumer backlash was significant — social media complaints, some businesses reporting 300-500% electricity cost increases. This is directly linked to the 2023 watershed (fuel subsidy removal) — both events hit simultaneously and accelerated solar adoption.

section_10_five_year_summary.five_year_insight: The five-year picture is striking: revenue 4x from 2020 to 2024 entirely through tariff increases. Operating losses converted to profit in 2022. But net current liabilities have NEVER improved — they range between ₦110-123 billion every year. Revenue growth is not translating into balance sheet repair. The structural problem is unchanged: AEDC cannot collect enough cash to cover its energy costs.

section_11_employee_data.ceo_change_2024: Christopher Ezeafulukwe resigned July 29 2024. Chijioke Okwuokenye appointed Ag. MD/CEO July 22 2024. Leadership instability continues — three majority owners since 2013, now third CEO regime.
section_11_employee_data.om_agreement_with_parent: Jeolan International earns 2% of collections as fixed fee + performance-based management fee. ₦5.8bn accrued in 2024 but not paid.

kann_force_majeure_2015.context: KANN (then-owner) declared force majeure citing inability to operate under prevailing market conditions. This was a direct signal that the privatisation structure was broken from year two. Documented in aedc_2015.json.

section_12_force_majeure_history.2024_force_majeure_events: None declared in 2024. Company has stabilised operationally under Jeolan despite financial fragility.

regulatory_fine_provision.status: Case withdrawn from court. Provision maintained.

section_13_cbn_intervention_fund.repayment_status: Ongoing — being repaid from collections at 81-month tenor. 2024 principal repayment ₦7.4bn.
section_13_cbn_intervention_fund.strategic_note: CBN has been the de facto equity provider and liquidity backstop for AEDC since 2018. Without NEMSF, AEDC would have collapsed. The facility is now entering repayment phase — increased cash drain on a company still structurally loss-making at the operational level.
section_13_cbn_intervention_fund.nmmp_note: CBN-funded National Mass Metering Programme — ₦6bn used to procure and install meters. Directly increased metered customer base and collection efficiency.

section_14_nelmco_transition_liabilities.pre_completion_receivables_status: Deed of assignment signed Oct 2013 transferring all pre-completion receivables and liabilities to NELMCO. Reconciliation between AEDC and NELMCO NOT YET COMPLETED as at August 2025 — over 11 years post-privatisation.
section_14_nelmco_transition_liabilities.reconciliation_risk: If reconciliation reveals liabilities borne by AEDC, amounts will be recorded in period identified. Open-ended contingent risk.
section_14_nelmco_transition_liabilities.tcn_loan_note: TCN loan ₦4.1bn at 24% interest — borrowed to fund Letter of Credit for TEM market participation. Effectively a penalty for being too poor to provide collateral independently.

section_15_customer_contributed_assets.value_recognised_as_revenue_2022_note: Historically significant — consumers built AEDC's network for it
section_15_customer_contributed_assets.policy_change: NERC April 2023 order: meter costs funded by customers must be repaid via energy credits. No longer recognised as DISCO revenue. This protects consumers but creates growing MAP liability for DISCOs.
section_15_customer_contributed_assets.distribution_network_assets_contributed: Transformers, substations, distribution lines contributed by customers/communities — ₦31m in 2024 (down from ₦388m in 2023 of non-meter assets)
section_15_customer_contributed_assets.consumer_self_funding_note: Nigerian electricity consumers have been building and funding the distribution network for decades — paying for meters, transformers, and substations — with no equity stake in return. This is the hidden subsidy that props up DISCO economics.

band_a_tariff_shock.description: April 2024 Band A tariff increase. Commercial and upper-residential customers (highest consumption) faced 200-400% bill increases. This segment has highest purchasing power for solar alternatives.
band_a_tariff_shock.vitics_signal: STRONG — Band A customers are exactly the YouTube-searching, solar-considering demographic. April 2024 tariff shock directly correlates with YouTube solar search volume spikes in FCT, Niger, Kogi.

prepaid_share_declining.description: Prepaid electricity sales fell from 39.8% to 33.0% of total electricity revenue. If prepaid customers (who are metered) represent stable or growing numbers but falling share, it could mean prepaid customers are consuming less from the grid.
prepaid_share_declining.vitics_signal: MODERATE — lower grid consumption from metered/prepaid customers is consistent with solar self-generation supplementing or replacing grid consumption.

receivables_acceleration_vs_consumption.description: Gross receivables grew 47% while actual electricity sales grew 78% — driven almost entirely by tariff increase, not volume increase. If volume growth is moderate but tariff growth is high, unit consumption may actually be flat or declining.
receivables_acceleration_vs_consumption.vitics_signal: MODERATE — flat volume despite population and economic growth = demand destruction or off-grid substitution.

map_liability_explosion.description: MAP liability tripled from ₦5.5bn to ₦15.6bn. 14 MAP entities contracted. Customers are pre-funding their own meters at scale.
map_liability_explosion.vitics_signal: MODERATE — customers willing to pre-fund meters also have capital available. Same capital formation enables solar purchases.

band_a_tariff_as_solar_tipping_point.description: April 2024 Band A tariff created cost-of-grid vs cost-of-solar comparison that for many commercial users now favours solar on ROI basis.
band_a_tariff_as_solar_tipping_point.vitics_signal: STRONG — this is the 2024 equivalent of the 2023 fuel subsidy removal. Sequential shocks are creating a compounding solar adoption curve.

section_16_grid_defection_signals.collection_rate_improvement_paradox: Collections improved 63% by May 2025. But this may reflect Band A customers paying higher bills — NOT more customers paying. The unmetered low-tier base (₦157.7bn receivables) remains uncollectable. This means AEDC is extracting more from fewer customers who are also the most likely to go solar.

section_17_consumer_reality_assessment.consumer_impact_score_rationale: Severe negative impact. Worst tariff shock since privatisation. Band A customers in Abuja, Niger, Kogi, Nasarawa experienced largest single-year bill increases in the electricity sector's post-privatisation history.

direct_consumer_impacts.band_a_tariff_increase: April 2024 NERC order raised Band A to cost-reflective rates. Estimates suggest 200-400% effective bill increases for commercial users. Residential Band A customers similarly affected.
direct_consumer_impacts.band_b_to_e_customers: Lower band customers continue receiving subsidised (below cost-reflective) tariffs but face continued unreliable supply. No material improvement in supply hours.
direct_consumer_impacts.meter_funding_burden: Consumers continue to pre-fund meters via MAP scheme — ₦15.6bn outstanding. Government policy requires repayment via energy credits, but the upfront cash burden remains with consumers.
direct_consumer_impacts.estimated_billing_persistence: Unmetered customers (₦157.7bn receivables) still subject to estimated billing disputes. Estimated billing is one of the most-complained-about issues in Nigerian energy consumer discourse.
direct_consumer_impacts.tariff_shortfall_subsidy_paradox: ₦285bn tariff shortfall awarded in 2024 means lower-band customers are being cross-subsidised by the government — but they do not experience better service. The subsidy pays AEDC's NBET debt, not consumer service improvements.

positive_signals_for_consumers.metering_acceleration: 91,372 customers metered in 2024. Moving from estimated to actual billing is positive for transparent billing.
positive_signals_for_consumers.nmmp_completion: National Mass Metering Programme improved meter penetration in the franchise area.
positive_signals_for_consumers.map_reimbursement: Consumers who pre-funded meters are now receiving energy credits as repayment — policy improvement.
positive_signals_for_consumers.100pct_nbet_onem_settlement_may_2025: If AEDC can sustain full invoice settlement, market liquidity improves, potentially allowing more electricity to flow to consumers.

section_18_vitics_analysis_instructions.primary_vitics_signal: WATERSHED CONFIRMATION — 2024 AEDC data confirms that the April 2024 Band A tariff shock is the second consecutive year (after 2023 fuel subsidy removal) of a major cost-of-electricity shock driving solar adoption searches in the FCT/North-Central Nigeria corridor.
section_18_vitics_analysis_instructions.key_analytical_frame: Did this report change anything for the Nigerian energy consumer? YES — dramatically worse for Band A customers who now pay full cost-reflective tariffs. Marginally better for low-tier customers (continuing subsidy) but no service improvement. The consumer paying ₦100,000+ monthly electricity bills in 2024 is the same consumer searching YouTube for 'solar for office Abuja' and 'how to reduce AEDC bill'.

comparison_with_aedc_2015.revenue_growth: 2015: ₦42bn revenue. 2024: ₦602bn revenue — 14x growth in nominal terms. In real (inflation-adjusted) terms far less, but still massive.
comparison_with_aedc_2015.structural_problems_unchanged: 2015: loss-making, cannot collect from customers, consumers self-funding assets. 2024: nominally profitable, cannot collect from customers, consumers self-funding assets. Core structural dysfunction unchanged over 9 years.
comparison_with_aedc_2015.losses_trajectory: 2015: ₦41.97bn net loss. 2024: ₦781m profit. Nominal improvement but achieved through tariff increases and accounting (shortfall recognition), not operational transformation.
comparison_with_aedc_2015.going_concern: 2015: KPMG flagged going concern. 2024: EY clear on going concern — first clean bill in years. But accumulated losses remain and net current liabilities worsened.
comparison_with_aedc_2015.consumer_self_funding: 2015: Consumers donated ₦127m in assets. 2024: MAP liability ₦15.6bn — consumers pre-funded meters at scale. Consumer self-funding has grown 100x+ over 9 years.

youtube_search_term_connections.band_a_tariff_searches: AEDC new tariff 2024 / NERC Band A electricity bill Abuja / why is my AEDC bill so high
youtube_search_term_connections.solar_migration_searches: solar for office Abuja 2024 / how to reduce AEDC bill / solar panel price Abuja / AEDC solar alternative
youtube_search_term_connections.frustration_searches: AEDC complaint 2024 / estimated billing AEDC / AEDC meter request / AEDC customer care
youtube_search_term_connections.direct_to_solar: solar installation Abuja / inverter price Nigeria 2024 / lithium battery Nigeria / off-grid solar FCT

section_18_vitics_analysis_instructions.transition_note: 2024 AEDC report confirms the FCT/North-Central Nigeria solar transition is being driven by: (1) April 2024 Band A tariff shock — cost of grid now exceeds solar ROI threshold for commercial users. (2) 9 years of unresolved structural dysfunction confirming to consumers that the grid will never be reliable or affordable. (3) MAP scheme showing consumers have capital to invest in electricity infrastructure — the same capital formation that enables solar adoption. The consumer who pre-funded their meter in 2024 is the consumer who installs solar in 2025.
section_18_vitics_analysis_instructions.confidence_in_data: HIGH — audited by Ernst & Young with clean unmodified opinion. All figures reliable. Key risk: tariff shortfall revenue recognition methodology is complex and subject to regulatory interpretation.
section_18_vitics_analysis_instructions.reading_notes: Document is text-searchable PDF. All financial data extracted directly. No OCR errors. 80 pages fully read. Report signed August 13, 2025 — released approximately 7.5 months after period end (some delay vs. global standards but normal for Nigerian DISCOs).

sdg_7_affordable_clean_energy.note: Energy available but not affordable after Band A tariff. Clean dimension absent — grid power sourced from predominantly fossil fuel generation. Supply reliability no material improvement.

sdg_10_reduced_inequalities.note: Band A/Band B bifurcation entrenches inequality. Wealthy commercial users pay cost-reflective rates and likely go solar. Poor unmetered customers continue estimated billing. The tariff structure creates a two-tier energy apartheid.

sdg_8_decent_work_economic_growth.note: Businesses in FCT/Niger/Kogi/Nasarawa facing 200-400% electricity cost increases. SME viability threatened. Some large businesses accelerating solar to escape.

sdg_13_climate_action.note: Perverse positive: by making grid electricity unaffordable, AEDC's dysfunction is inadvertently accelerating rooftop solar adoption faster than any deliberate clean energy policy. The crisis is the transition catalyst.

sdg_5_gender_equality.note: Women disproportionately affected by unreliable electricity — household labour, small business operations, safety. Board gender ratio 1:2 women to men. No gender-disaggregated data on consumer impact.

section_20_document_quality.reading_method: Direct text extraction from PDF — document is text-searchable throughout
section_20_document_quality.data_completeness: COMPLETE — all financial statements, notes, five-year summary, and qualitative disclosures extracted
limitations: No operational data on actual units of electricity distributed (MWh/kWh) — prevents direct volume analysis
No customer count data — prevents per-customer revenue/debt calculation
No supply hours data — cannot quantify hours of power per day
Tariff shortfall accounting is complex; ₦285bn figure depends on NERC regulatory orders, not cash
